articles.title: How to Select the Right BPS LED Driver for Your Application
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting BPS LED drivers based on application requirements, covering topology selection, power calculations, and key parameter considerations.
content: Selecting the right LED driver is crucial for achieving optimal performance, efficiency, and reliability in your lighting application. This guide walks you through the key considerations when choosing from BPS's extensive LED driver portfolio.
BPS offers several LED driver topologies, each suited for different applications:
- Best for: Low to medium power applications (5W-60W)
- Advantages: High efficiency, simple design, lower cost
- Input voltage: Typically rectified AC or DC
- Applications: Downlights, panel lights, bulb replacements
- Best for: Applications requiring safety isolation
- Best for: Intelligent lighting with dimming and color control
- Applications: Smart home, hospitality, architectural lighting
- AC mains: Select drivers with universal input (85-305VAC)
- DC systems: Match the driver to your DC bus voltage
- Consider line variations and transient conditions
- LED forward voltage × LED current × number of strings
- Consider temperature effects on LED forward voltage
- PWM dimming: Precise control, digital systems
- Smart control: I2C interface for advanced features
- Calculate power dissipation for thermal design
For retrofit bulbs and downlights, the BP2861 offers an excellent balance of cost and performance. Its compact SOP-8 package fits space-constrained designs while delivering up to 93% efficiency.
Office and retail applications benefit from the BP2865's higher power capability and excellent dimming performance. The wide input range accommodates varying grid conditions.
The BP5758D provides five independent channels with I2C control, ideal for tunable white and RGB applications. Its 12-bit PWM resolution ensures smooth, flicker-free dimming.
- [ ] Verify input voltage range covers worst-case conditions
- [ ] Confirm output power rating exceeds maximum load by 20%
- [ ] Check dimming compatibility with control system
- [ ] Validate thermal performance at maximum ambient
- [ ] Review protection features match application needs
- [ ] Confirm package size fits mechanical constraints
Our FAE team is available to review your design requirements and provide personalized recommendations. Contact us with your application specifications for detailed guidance.

faeInsights.insight: After supporting hundreds of LED driver designs, I've found that the most common selection mistake is overlooking thermal performance at minimum dimming levels. At low brightness, efficiency drops and the driver runs hotter relative to output power. Always verify thermal design at minimum dimming, not just full brightness.
faeInsights.logic: Start with your LED specifications - forward voltage and current requirements. Calculate total power and add 20% margin. Then consider your input source and dimming needs. Finally, verify that your mechanical constraints accommodate the chosen package and thermal requirements.
practicalTips: Use the maximum LED forward voltage at lowest temperature for worst-case power calculation
Verify dimming performance with your actual control system before mass production
Include adequate copper area for thermal dissipation in your PCB layout
Test EMI performance early in the design process
Consider future power requirements - selecting a slightly higher power rating provides upgrade flexibility
faeInsights.insightLogic: The key insight is that LED driver selection must consider the complete operating envelope, not just nominal conditions. Thermal performance, EMI compliance, and dimming behavior all vary across the operating range.

customerCases.problem: The customer was experiencing inconsistent dimming performance across different installations and needed a standardized driver solution that would work reliably with various control systems.
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis revealed that the previous driver selection didn't account for the wide variation in 0-10V control signals across different manufacturers. Some controls sourced current while others required sinking, causing compatibility issues.
customerCases.solution: Recommended the BP2865 with its flexible dimming interface that supports both sourcing and sinking configurations. Provided reference circuits for each control type and tested compatibility with major control brands.
customerCases.results: Standardized on BP2865 across all panel light products. Dimming compatibility issues eliminated, reducing field support calls by 80%. Product line achieved consistent 4.5+ star ratings for dimming performance.
customerCases.feedback: Standardized on BP2865 across all panel light products. Dimming compatibility issues eliminated, reducing field support calls by 80%. Product line achieved consistent 4.5+ star ratings for dimming performance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between isolated and non-isolated LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Isolated drivers use a transformer to provide galvanic isolation between input and output, essential for safety in high-voltage applications. Non-isolated buck drivers offer higher efficiency and lower cost but require careful system design to ensure safety. Choose isolated for universal AC input applications where user contact with LEDs is possible; choose non-isolated for embedded applications with proper enclosure.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our safety specialists if you're unsure about isolation requirements for your specific application.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the right LED driver power rating?
faqs.answer: Calculate LED power as: Number of LEDs × Forward Voltage × LED Current. Then add 20% margin for voltage variations and aging. For example, 10 LEDs at 3V and 300mA = 9W; with 20% margin, select a 12W or higher driver. Consider temperature effects - LED forward voltage decreases at high temperature, potentially increasing current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our online power calculator or contact our FAE team for complex multi-string designs.

faqs.question: Which BPS driver is best for smart home lighting?
faqs.answer: For smart home applications requiring dimming and color control, the BP5758D is ideal with its 5-channel PWM control and I2C interface. It integrates directly with microcontrollers for WiFi, Zigbee, or Bluetooth connectivity. For simpler dimmable white applications, the BP2865 with 0-10V or PWM dimming provides a cost-effective solution.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our smart lighting reference designs or request a consultation for your specific connectivity requirements.

faqs.question: What protection features should I look for in an LED driver?
faqs.answer: Essential protections include: Over-Voltage Protection (OVP) for open-LED conditions, Over-Current Protection (OCP) for fault conditions, Over-Temperature Protection (OTP) for thermal management, and Short-Circuit Protection (SCP) for output faults. BPS drivers include all these protections integrated, reducing external component count and improving reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our protection features comparison guide for detailed specifications of each driver's protection capabilities.

faqs.question: How important is power factor for LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Power factor (PF) is crucial for commercial installations where utilities charge penalties for low PF. A PF > 0.9 is typically required. BPS drivers achieve high power factor through active power factor correction or valley-fill circuits. High PF also reduces line current, allowing more fixtures per circuit breaker.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check your local utility requirements and select drivers with appropriate PF specifications for your installation.

faqs.question: Can I use BPS drivers for outdoor lighting applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, BPS drivers are suitable for outdoor lighting when properly implemented. Consider the BP3319 or BP3339 isolated flyback drivers for street and area lighting. Ensure adequate surge protection (typically 4kV or higher), proper enclosure sealing (IP65 or better), and thermal design for high ambient temperatures. BPS drivers operate reliably up to 85°C ambient.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our outdoor lighting specialists for reference designs including surge protection and thermal management.

articles.title: PCB Layout Guidelines for BPS LED Drivers
articles.summary: Essential PCB layout guidelines for BPS LED drivers covering component placement, trace routing, thermal management, and EMI reduction techniques.
content: Proper PCB layout is critical for LED driver performance, efficiency, and EMI compliance. This guide provides practical layout guidelines for BPS LED driver designs.
Place input components in this order from the input connector:
Keep the input capacitor as close as possible to the IC VCC pin. This minimizes switching noise on the power rail and ensures stable operation.
The switching loop (input cap → IC → inductor → output cap → ground) should be as small as possible:
- Use short, wide traces for high-current paths
- Place inductor adjacent to the switching node
- Minimize loop area to reduce EMI radiation
Feedback traces are sensitive to noise:
- Route away from switching nodes and inductors
- Use ground plane shielding when possible
- Use 1oz copper minimum; 2oz preferred for high current
- Calculate trace width based on current and temperature rise
- Use multiple vias for layer transitions
- Separate power ground and signal ground
- Connect grounds at a single point near the IC
- Use ground planes for heat dissipation
LED drivers generate heat that must be managed:
- Provide copper area under the IC for heat sinking
- Use thermal vias to transfer heat to inner layers
- Consider external heat sinks for high-power applications
- Maintain adequate spacing from heat-sensitive components
- Place vias in a grid pattern under the IC
- Connect to ground planes on multiple layers
- Fill or tent vias to prevent solder wicking
Proper input filtering reduces conducted EMI:
- Include common-mode choke for differential noise
- Add X capacitor across line and neutral
- Use Y capacitors for common-mode noise
- Place filter components close to input connector
- Place RC snubber across switching node and ground
- Optimize values based on actual measurements
- Consider metal shield over switching section
- Implement Faraday cage for high-frequency noise
- Minimize loop: Input cap → IC → Inductor → Output cap
- Place freewheeling diode close to inductor and IC
- Keep feedback trace away from switching node
- Use Kelvin connection for current sensing
- Maintain isolation spacing between primary and secondary
- Place clamp circuit close to transformer primary
- [ ] Review all high-current paths for adequate width
- [ ] Verify thermal via placement and density
- [ ] Confirm feedback trace routing away from noise sources
- [ ] Validate ground connections and star grounding

faeInsights.insight: In my experience reviewing hundreds of LED driver layouts, the most common issue is inadequate attention to the switching loop area. A large switching loop acts as an antenna, radiating EMI and reducing efficiency. I always recommend designers start layout by placing the input capacitor, IC, and inductor first, optimizing their placement before adding other components.
faeInsights.logic: Think of current flow as water - it follows the path of least resistance and creates ripples (EMI) when forced through narrow channels. Wide, short traces for high current; sensitive signals protected from noise; heat provided an escape path through copper and vias.
practicalTips: Start layout with the switching loop - everything else follows
Use copper pours for high-current paths rather than traces
Place thermal vias before routing other signals
Keep sensitive analog traces on inner layers with ground planes above and below
Always prototype and measure EMI before finalizing production layout
faeInsights.insightLogic: The fundamental principle is that good layout is about controlling electromagnetic fields. Every current loop is an antenna; every gap in ground is a slot radiator. Minimize loops, maintain continuous ground, and provide controlled paths for heat and current.

customerCases.problem: The customer's initial design failed EMI compliance testing, with excessive conducted emissions in the 150kHz-1MHz range. Multiple layout iterations were consuming development time.
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis of the layout revealed a large switching loop area and inadequate input filtering. The input capacitor was placed far from the IC, creating a large antenna loop radiating switching noise.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned layout following BPS guidelines: moved input capacitor adjacent to IC, minimized switching loop area, added proper input filter with common-mode choke, and implemented star grounding. Added snubber circuit across switching node.
customerCases.results: EMI performance improved by 15dB, easily meeting EN55015 Class B limits. The optimized layout also improved efficiency by 2% and reduced IC operating temperature by 12°C. Design passed certification on first submission.
customerCases.feedback: EMI performance improved by 15dB, easily meeting EN55015 Class B limits. The optimized layout also improved efficiency by 2% and reduced IC operating temperature by 12°C. Design passed certification on first submission.

faqs.question: What is the most critical layout consideration for LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Minimizing the switching loop area is the most critical factor. The loop formed by the input capacitor, IC, inductor, and output capacitor acts as an antenna radiating EMI. Keep this loop as small as possible by placing these components adjacent to each other with short, wide traces. This single layout decision has more impact on EMI and efficiency than any other factor.
faqs.decisionGuide: Download our switching loop optimization guide with example layouts for different topologies.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the right trace width for power paths?
faqs.answer: Trace width depends on current, copper thickness, and acceptable temperature rise. For 1oz copper, use approximately 0.3mm width per amp for 10°C rise. For LED driver applications, aim for 20°C maximum temperature rise. Online calculators can provide precise values. When in doubt, use wider traces - there's no penalty for over-designing power paths.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our trace width calculator or IPC-2152 standard for precise calculations based on your specific requirements.

faqs.question: What is the best grounding strategy for LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Implement a star grounding scheme with a single connection point between power ground and signal ground. Place this connection near the IC. Use separate ground planes that connect at one point. This prevents switching currents from flowing through sensitive signal ground references and causing noise issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our grounding application note with illustrated examples of proper star grounding implementation.

faqs.question: How many thermal vias do I need for adequate heat dissipation?
faqs.answer: Thermal via requirements depend on power dissipation and ambient conditions. As a starting point, use a 3x3 grid of 0.3mm vias under the IC for applications up to 1W dissipation. For higher power, increase to 4x4 or 5x5 grids. Connect vias to ground planes on multiple layers. Measure actual IC temperature in the application and adjust via density if needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our thermal specialists for thermal modeling and optimization recommendations for your specific design.

faqs.question: Should I use a 2-layer or 4-layer PCB for LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Two-layer PCBs are sufficient for simple designs under 20W with relaxed EMI requirements. Four-layer boards are recommended for higher power, strict EMI requirements, or complex designs. The dedicated ground and power planes in 4-layer designs improve EMI performance and thermal management. Cost difference is typically small compared to overall product value.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 4-layer for designs over 30W or commercial products requiring EMI compliance. Use 2-layer only for cost-sensitive, low-power consumer products.

faqs.question: How do I prevent solder wicking in thermal vias?
faqs.answer: Solder wicking in thermal vias can cause assembly issues and reduce thermal performance. Solutions include: using smaller via diameters (0.2-0.3mm), capping vias with solder mask, filling vias with conductive epoxy, or using tenting on the component side. The best approach depends on your assembly process and thermal requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consult with your PCB manufacturer about their capabilities and recommendations for via treatment in your specific application.

articles.title: Dimming Techniques and Implementation for BPS LED Drivers
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to LED dimming techniques including 0-10V analog, PWM digital, and smart control implementation with BPS drivers.
content: Dimming is a key feature in modern LED lighting systems, providing energy savings, ambiance control, and extended LED lifetime. This application note covers dimming techniques and implementation with BPS LED drivers.
The industry standard for commercial lighting:
- Connect dimming wires to DIM+ and DIM- pins
- BPS drivers support both sourcing and sinking types
Digital control method ideal for smart systems:
- Apply PWM signal to dimming pin
- Connect SDA and SCL to microcontroller
- Support for multiple devices on bus
- Balance against EMI and switching losses
- Add RC filter on dimming input
The human eye perceives brightness logarithmically:
- Gamma correction needed for smooth perception
Apply gamma correction for smooth dimming:
- BPS smart controllers support gamma tables
- Use signal amplifiers for larger systems
- Consider voltage drop in long runs
- Prevents LED turn-off at low levels

faeInsights.insight: Dimming compatibility issues are the most common field problems I encounter. The root cause is usually a mismatch between the driver's dimming interface type and the control system's output type. Always verify whether your control system sources or sinks current, and select the appropriate driver configuration. Testing with the actual control system before mass production is essential.
faeInsights.logic: Think of dimming as a conversation between driver and controller - they need to speak the same language. Verify the interface type, signal levels, and timing. Test across the full dimming range, not just full-on and full-off. Consider worst-case conditions including long cable runs and multiple fixtures.
practicalTips: Always test dimming with the actual control system before production
Use shielded cable for dimming signals in noisy environments
Implement soft-start to improve perceived quality and LED lifetime
Consider gamma correction for perceived linear dimming
Document dimming compatibility in product specifications
faeInsights.insightLogic: The key to successful dimming implementation is understanding that it's a system-level consideration, not just a driver feature. The control system, wiring, driver, and LEDs must all work together harmoniously.

customerCases.problem: The customer reported inconsistent dimming performance across 200+ fixtures, with some fixtures flickering at low levels and others not dimming smoothly. The system used a centralized 0-10V control system.
customerCases.diagnosis: Investigation revealed that the control system sourced current, but some drivers were configured for sinking operation. Additionally, the long cable runs caused voltage drop, reducing the effective dimming range at distant fixtures.
customerCases.solution: Standardized on BP2865 drivers configured for sourcing operation to match the control system. Implemented signal amplifiers every 50 fixtures to maintain voltage levels. Added RC filtering at each driver input to reduce noise pickup.
customerCases.results: Dimming performance became consistent across all fixtures. Flicker was eliminated even at 1% brightness. The customer achieved the desired energy savings and user satisfaction with smooth, reliable dimming control.
customerCases.feedback: Dimming performance became consistent across all fixtures. Flicker was eliminated even at 1% brightness. The customer achieved the desired energy savings and user satisfaction with smooth, reliable dimming control.

faqs.question: What causes LED flicker during dimming and how do I prevent it?
faqs.answer: Flicker is caused by insufficient PWM frequency, excessive current ripple, or noise on the dimming signal. Prevent flicker by using PWM frequencies above 1kHz, implementing proper output filtering with adequate capacitance, and adding RC filters on dimming inputs. For analog dimming, use shielded cables and keep dimming wires away from switching noise sources.
faqs.decisionGuide: Download our flicker-free dimming application note with detailed filter design calculations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sourcing and sinking dimming interfaces?
faqs.answer: Sourcing controls provide current to the driver's dimming input, while sinking controls accept current from the driver. BPS drivers can be configured for either type by component selection. North American systems typically use sourcing controls, while European systems often use sinking. Using the wrong configuration results in no dimming or reverse operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to verify the correct configuration for your specific control system.

faqs.question: How do I achieve smooth dimming from 100% to 1%?
faqs.answer: Smooth dimming requires three elements: sufficient PWM resolution (12-bit recommended), proper gamma correction to match human eye response, and stable current regulation at low levels. BPS smart controllers provide 12-bit resolution and support gamma tables. For analog dimming, ensure clean control signals and adequate driver current regulation range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our gamma correction calculator and reference code for optimal dimming curves.

faqs.question: Can I connect multiple fixtures to one dimming control?
faqs.answer: Yes, multiple fixtures can share a dimming control, but the total number is limited by the control system's drive capability. Typical limits are 50-100 fixtures for 0-10V systems. For larger installations, use signal amplifiers or repeater modules. Consider voltage drop in long cable runs, which can reduce dimming range at distant fixtures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate your system capacity using our dimming system design guide or contact us for large installation support.

faqs.question: What is the minimum dimming level for BPS LED drivers?
faqs.answer: BPS drivers support dimming down to 1% or lower, depending on the specific model. The BP2865 achieves 1% minimum with stable operation, while the BP5758D smart controller can dim to 0.1% with its 12-bit resolution. Actual minimum levels depend on LED characteristics and thermal design. Some applications may prefer setting a 5-10% minimum to ensure stable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review individual product datasheets for specific dimming range specifications.

faqs.question: How do I integrate BPS drivers with smart home systems?
faqs.answer: Integration depends on your smart home platform. For basic dimming, use 0-10V or PWM interfaces connected to a smart relay or dimmer module. For advanced features, the BP5758D's I2C interface connects to microcontrollers with WiFi, Zigbee, or Bluetooth. We provide reference designs for popular platforms including Alexa, Google Home, and HomeKit integration.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request our smart home integration guide with reference designs for your specific platform.

articles.title: Thermal Design and Management for LED Driver Applications
articles.summary: Comprehensive thermal design guide for LED driver applications covering power loss calculation, heat sinking, thermal modeling, and reliability considerations.
content: Thermal management is critical for LED driver reliability and lifetime. This guide provides practical thermal design methodologies for BPS LED driver applications.
- **Conduction losses**: I²R losses in MOSFETs and inductors
- **Switching losses**: Energy lost during transistor switching
- **Gate drive losses**: Power to charge/discharge MOSFET gates
Where: Pout = LED power, η = efficiency
Efficiency typically decreases at high temperatures:
- θJA: Junction to ambient (total thermal resistance)
Where: Tj = junction temp, Ta = ambient temp
Note: Actual θJA depends heavily on PCB layout and copper area.
Copper on PCB acts as a heatsink:
- Use maximum copper area under IC
- Extend copper to board edges when possible
- Connect to inner layers with thermal vias
Thermal vias transfer heat to inner layers:
- Place IC in board center for heat spreading
- Keep high-power components away from IC
- Provide air flow paths when possible
### When to Use External Heat Sinks
2. Estimate effective θJA from copper area

faeInsights.insight: The most common thermal design error I see is designing for nominal conditions rather than worst-case. LED drivers often operate in enclosed fixtures with limited airflow, at high ambient temperatures, and at minimum dimming where efficiency is lowest. Always design for maximum ambient temperature, minimum dimming level, and highest input voltage - these are the conditions that produce maximum junction temperature.
faeInsights.logic: Think of thermal design as creating a path for heat to escape. Heat flows from junction to case, through thermal interface material, to heat sink or PCB, and finally to ambient air. Each interface has thermal resistance; minimize each resistance and ensure the complete path can handle the heat flow.
practicalTips: Always design for worst-case: max ambient, min dimming, max input voltage
Use thermal vias liberally - they're cheap insurance against overheating
Measure actual temperatures in the final enclosure, not open air
Consider seasonal variations - attics and enclosed spaces get very hot
Plan for end-of-life - thermal interface materials degrade over time
faeInsights.insightLogic: Thermal design is about managing energy flow. The heat generated in the silicon must have a low-resistance path to the environment. Any bottleneck in this path creates a temperature rise. Design the complete thermal path, not just individual components.

customerCases.problem: The customer experienced premature driver failures in desert installations where ambient temperatures reached 70°C. Standard thermal design was insufficient for these extreme conditions.
customerCases.diagnosis: Thermal analysis revealed junction temperatures exceeding 140°C under worst-case conditions. The original design assumed 40°C ambient, typical for indoor applications, but outdoor fixtures in direct sunlight experience much higher temperatures.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with enhanced thermal management: increased copper area to 50cm², added 25 thermal vias, implemented external aluminum heat sink bonded to PCB, and selected higher-efficiency BP2865 driver to reduce power dissipation.
customerCases.results: Junction temperature reduced to 110°C at 70°C ambient. Field failure rate dropped from 8% to 0.2% annually. Design successfully passed 10-year accelerated life testing, confirming suitability for harsh outdoor environments.
customerCases.feedback: Junction temperature reduced to 110°C at 70°C ambient. Field failure rate dropped from 8% to 0.2% annually. Design successfully passed 10-year accelerated life testing, confirming suitability for harsh outdoor environments.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the maximum allowable power dissipation for a BPS LED driver?
faqs.answer: Calculate using: Pmax = (Tj_max - Ta) / θJA. For BPS drivers, Tj_max is typically 125°C recommended, 150°C absolute maximum. θJA depends on your PCB layout - use 80°C/W for minimal copper, 45°C/W for optimized layout with thermal vias. Example: At 50°C ambient with θJA = 50°C/W, Pmax = (125 - 50) / 50 = 1.5W. Always include 20% safety margin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our thermal calculator tool or contact our FAE team for detailed thermal modeling of your specific design.

faqs.question: What is the most effective way to improve thermal performance?
faqs.answer: The most effective improvement is increasing PCB copper area under and around the IC. Doubling copper area can reduce θJA by 30-50%. Next, add thermal vias to transfer heat to inner layers. For high-power applications, add an external heat sink bonded to the PCB. Improving efficiency by selecting a better topology or component also reduces heat generation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Download our thermal optimization guide with before/after examples and measurement data.

faqs.question: How does dimming affect thermal performance?
faqs.answer: Dimming affects thermal performance non-linearly. At low dimming levels, efficiency typically decreases because fixed losses (gate drive, control circuit) become a larger percentage of output power. Additionally, reduced LED current may decrease LED forward voltage, potentially increasing driver losses. Always verify thermal performance at minimum dimming, not just full brightness.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our dimming thermal analysis application note with efficiency curves across the dimming range.

faqs.question: What thermal interface material should I use between the IC and heat sink?
faqs.answer: For PCB-level thermal management, thermal vias provide the best heat transfer. For external heat sinks, use thermal grease (0.1-0.5°C-cm²/W) for best performance, or thermal pads (0.5-3°C-cm²/W) for easier assembly. Gap-filling materials are needed if the gap exceeds 0.1mm. Consider electrical isolation requirements - some materials provide both thermal and electrical isolation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our thermal specialists for material recommendations based on your specific mechanical design.

faqs.question: How do I measure IC junction temperature in my design?
faqs.answer: Direct junction temperature measurement requires specialized equipment. Practical methods include: measuring case temperature with thermocouple and adding calculated temperature rise (Tj = Tcase + Ploss × θJC), using the IC's internal temperature sensor if available, or measuring sensitive electrical parameters that vary with temperature. For verification, use IR thermal camera to identify hot spots.
faqs.decisionGuide: Our application note on temperature measurement techniques provides detailed procedures and accuracy considerations.

faqs.question: What ambient temperature should I design for?
faqs.answer: Design for the maximum ambient temperature your product will experience. Indoor applications: typically 40-50°C. Enclosed fixtures: add 10-20°C to ambient. Outdoor applications: consider 60-70°C with direct sun exposure. Industrial environments: may reach 85°C. Always include margin for seasonal variations, installation variations, and end-of-life degradation. When in doubt, design for 85°C ambient.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our environmental design guidelines for different application types and installation conditions.

seoTitle: BPS Technical Support | LED Driver Design Resources | LiTong Electronics
seoDescription: BPS LED driver technical support, application notes, design guides, and FAQs. Authorized distributor providing comprehensive engineering assistance.

faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive technical support for BPS products including application engineering, design reviews, reference designs, and troubleshooting assistance. Our FAE team has extensive experience with LED driver design and can help optimize your application.

faqs.question: Where can I find BPS reference designs and application notes?
faqs.answer: Reference designs and application notes are available through LiTong Electronics. We provide complete design packages including schematics, PCB layouts, BOMs, and test reports for typical LED lighting applications.

faqs.answer: Samples can be requested through LiTong Electronics. We maintain inventory of popular BPS devices for quick sample delivery. Contact our sales team with your requirements for sample availability and lead times.

We provide certificates of authenticity and can verify product origin through our quality management system. Avoid counterfeit products by purchasing only from authorized distributors.

faqs.question: Does LiTong offer volume pricing for BPS products?

Contact our sales team with your forecasted volumes for customized pricing proposals. We also offer scheduled delivery programs to optimize your inventory management.

faqs.question: What is the typical lead time for BPS products?
faqs.answer: Standard lead time for BPS products is 4-6 weeks for production quantities. Samples are typically available from stock with 1-2 week delivery. For large volume orders or special requirements, please contact our sales team for specific lead time information.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide design review services for BPS applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides comprehensive design review services including schematic review, PCB layout analysis, thermal modeling, and EMI pre-compliance testing. Our FAE team can identify potential issues early and recommend optimizations to ensure successful implementation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Submit your design for review through our technical support portal.

faqs.question: What warranty does LiTong provide for BPS products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides a standard 2-year warranty for all BPS products sold. The warranty covers manufacturing defects and ensures products meet published specifications. Extended warranty options are available for specific applications. Please refer to our terms and conditions for complete warranty details.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our warranty terms or contact sales for extended warranty options.
